subtopics: 2-D String DP $\cdot$ 0/1 Knapsack $\cdot$ State Machines $\cdot$ Interval DP $\cdot$ Regex Matching

blocks.content: **Longest Common Subsequence.** Given strings \texttt{text1} and \texttt{text2}, return the length of their longest common subsequence (LCS). A subsequence need not be contiguous.
**Edit Distance.** Return the minimum number of insert, delete, or replace operations to convert \texttt{word1} into \texttt{word2}.

**Interleaving String.** Given \texttt{s1}, \texttt{s2}, \texttt{s3}, return \texttt{True} iff \texttt{s3} is formed by interleaving \texttt{s1} and \texttt{s2} (preserving each string's character order).
**Partition Equal Subset Sum.** Given an integer array \texttt{nums}, return \texttt{True} iff it can be split into two subsets with equal sum.
blocks.solution:
**Reduction to 0/1 knapsack:** Total sum must be even; target $= \text{total}/2$. Find if any subset sums to \texttt{target}. Each element used at most once (0/1). **State:** $dp[j]$ = \texttt{True} iff a subset of elements seen so far sums to $j$. **Key:** Iterate $j$ *backwards* (right to left) to ensure each element is used at most once. \begin{lstlisting} def canPartition(nums: list[int]) -> bool: total = sum(nums) if total % 2: return False target = total // 2 dp = [False] * (target + 1) dp[0] = True # empty subset sums to 0 for n in nums: for j in range(target, n - 1, -1): # BACKWARDS: 0/1 knapsack dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j - n] return dp[target] \end{lstlisting} **Complexity:** $O(n\cdot\text{target})$ time, $O(\text{target})$ space. **0/1 vs unbounded knapsack (the critical loop direction):**
TypeInner loop directionEffect
0/1 knapsack (each item once)$j$ decreasingitem used at most once
Unbounded knapsack (reuse ok)$j$ increasingitem reusable

sections.title: State Machine DP and Interval DP

blocks.content: **Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown.** After selling, you must wait 1 day before buying again. Find the maximum profit.
**Burst Balloons.** Given balloon values \texttt{nums}, bursting balloon $i$ earns $\texttt{nums}[i-1]\times\texttt{nums}[i]\times\texttt{nums}[i+1]$ coins. Maximise total coins.
blocks.solution:
**Key insight:** Think in reverse. Instead of choosing which balloon to burst *first*, ask: which balloon is burst *last* in the range $(\texttt{left}, \texttt{right})$?

**Padding:** Prepend and append 1: $\texttt{nums}= [1] + \texttt{nums}+ [1]$.

**State:** $dp[\texttt{left}][\texttt{right}]$ = max coins from bursting all balloons strictly between indices \texttt{left} and \texttt{right} (exclusive endpoints are never burst in this subproblem).

**Recurrence:** For each candidate “last balloon” $k$ in $(\texttt{left}, \texttt{right})$: \[dp[l][r] = \max_{k}\bigl(\texttt{nums}[l]\times\texttt{nums}[k] \times\texttt{nums}[r] + dp[l][k] + dp[k][r]\bigr)\]

\begin{lstlisting}
def maxCoins(nums: list[int]) -> int:
    nums = [1] + nums + [1]        # sentinel boundaries
    n = len(nums)
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]
    # iterate over window sizes
    for length in range(2, n):     # window length (at least 1 interior)
        for left in range(0, n - length):
            right = left + length
            for k in range(left + 1, right):   # k is last burst
                coins = (nums[left] * nums[k] * nums[right]
                         + dp[left][k] + dp[k][right])
                dp[left][right] = max(dp[left][right], coins)
    return dp[0][n - 1]
\end{lstlisting}

**Complexity:** $O(n^{3})$ time, $O(n^{2})$ space.

**Why “last burst” works:** When $k$ is burst last in $(\texttt{left}, \texttt{right})$, its neighbours are exactly \texttt{left} and \texttt{right} (all others already gone). This avoids the dependency problem of “burst first” reasoning.

blocks.content: **Regular Expression Matching.** Implement matching for \texttt{.} (any char) and \texttt{*} (zero or more of preceding element). Return \texttt{True} iff \texttt{p} matches the entire string \texttt{s}.
**Distinct Subsequences.** Return the number of distinct ways \texttt{t} appears as a subsequence of \texttt{s}.
